chapters.title: Vol-016, Chapter 1: Three Months After
chapters.text:
The monitoring data arrived at 0614 on a Tuesday, flagged by the fixed-array network's automated anomaly detection as a significant change in the 65-meter signal at the Eastern Field Calibration coordinate. The automated flag went to Lian's terminal first, then — per the relay protocol she had set up three months ago, immediately after returning the instrument to the hub — to Corin's.

Corin read the flag at 0620. He was in the investigation office, already at his desk, working through the oversight committee's questions from the previous week's briefing. He set that work aside and read the flag twice.

*Signal at 65m coordinate: reorganization pattern. Prior state: recovery. Current state: pre-transmission. Transition logged at 0611:42.*

He sent a relay to Lian: *I saw it.*

Her response came in forty seconds: *I'm at the hub. Come now if you can.*

---

Lian had been tracking Sable's 65-meter signal continuously since the instrument returned to the hub. The monitoring setup was not complex — the hub's fixed-array network had been recalibrated after the field deployment to include the coordinate as a specific monitoring point, with the signal-analysis software reading the array's depth estimates at the coordinate's location and translating them into the Layer-approximation readings that were the best the fixed arrays could provide. Not the same as the instrument's direct cable read. The fixed arrays couldn't reach 65 meters. But they could read the substrate at 28 meters and extrapolate the Layer-edge signal at approximately 40 meters, and from the Layer-edge signal, Lian had built a proxy measurement for the 65-meter signal's general character. Good enough to track recovery versus pre-transmission states. Not good enough to read Sable's harmonic envelope in detail.

Good enough to know she was preparing to speak again.

"The transition happened at 0611," Lian said when Corin arrived at the hub. She was at the fixed-array display station. "The recovery pattern has been consistent since we returned — the attenuated oscillation, the flat character. Three hours ago, the pattern began to reorganize. By 0611 it had crossed the threshold for the pre-transmission state profile." She pulled up the comparison: the 65-meter signal from before the first contact sequence (baseline), the recovery pattern (post-transmission), and the current reading (pre-transmission). The current reading was not as strong as the pre-first-contact baseline. She had less capacity now than she'd had before the first transmissions. But she was in the posture of preparation. "She's recovered enough to transmit again."

Corin looked at the comparison. The pre-transmission profile was unmistakable against the recovery's flatness. "How long before she attempts transmission?"

"I don't know. The previous sequence — the posture appeared and the first fragment arrived within thirty hours. But she had more capacity then. The recovery from this lower baseline may produce a longer preparation phase before she's ready to transmit, or a shorter one if what she has to transmit is more compressed." Lian paused. "We need to go back to the coordinate. The instrument at depth is the only way to receive at the resolution we need. The fixed arrays can detect the state change but they can't decode a Reckoner-format compressed signal at 65 meters."

"How quickly can you prepare the instrument?"

"It's been in standby calibration since we returned. I've been maintaining it." She turned from the display. "Two days for the bore team to mobilize. One day to prepare the field equipment. Three days from now we can be at the coordinate with the cable at depth."

"Contact Petra for the bore team. I'll notify the oversight committee per the protocol." He paused. "And I'll relay to Iris — she'll want to be there."

---

They were at the coordinate on day four. The bore took six hours. The cable went in at 1540. By 1630 the cable was at 65 meters and the instrument was reading.

The 65-meter signal: pre-transmission state, confirmed on the direct cable read. Stronger than the fixed-array proxy had suggested — Sable had recovered more than the hub's extrapolation had captured, because the fixed arrays' resolution at depth was limited. She was still well below her original baseline, but she was in the pre-transmission state and the signal's character was more organized than the previous sequence's beginning.

"She's ready," Lian said.

Lian activated the sustained-contact mode — the modification she had built into the instrument over the three months since the first deployment. Not single transmissions but a continuous open channel: the recognition frequency pulsed at thirty-second intervals, the instrument's transmission circuit running a slow regular beat, a sustained signal to tell Sable that the instrument was present and receiving. The previous sequence had used single transmissions with two-minute intervals. This modification created a continuous presence rather than intermittent contact.

The modification had required rebuilding the transmission circuit's timing mechanism. The original pre-Reckoner circuit was designed for manual operation — the operator activated the transmission, the circuit pulsed once, the operator waited and activated again. There was no automated pulse capability in the original design. Lian had added a secondary timing module, built from current components, that ran the recognition frequency pulse at a fixed interval without operator intervention. The timing module could be set between fifteen and ninety seconds per pulse. She had run bench tests at thirty-second intervals for two weeks before the second deployment, confirming the module's stability under the generator's power output conditions. The modification added 0.3 kilograms to the instrument's surface unit and required a dedicated power circuit separate from the sensor's reception system, so that the transmission circuit's regular pulses did not introduce interference into the incoming signal readings. This had been the hardest part of the design — the isolation between transmission and reception within the same instrument housing. Lian had solved it with a switching relay that opened the transmission circuit in the 200-millisecond window before each scheduled reading, then closed it again after. The relay operated faster than the instrument's sampling interval. The readings were clean.

The 65-meter signal responded to the first pulse of the sustained contact: a small reorganization, not the full pre-transmission posture shift, but a directed orientation. Acknowledgment. She knew the instrument was there.

"Holding," Lian said. She set the sustained-contact mode to automatic and stepped back from the transmission panel. "We wait."

The first fragment of the second transmission sequence arrived eight hours later, at 0012 on the second day at the coordinate. Corin was asleep in the field shelter. Lian was awake — she had set a signal-threshold alert for the transition from pre-transmission posture to active transmission, and the alert woke her at 0009. She was at the instrument when the transmission began.

The fragment ran for eight minutes. Reckoner-format compressed signal, higher information density than the previous sequence's fragments — Sable had organized what she was transmitting more tightly, Lian thought, because she knew she had less capacity to spend and she was using it efficiently. Eight minutes of signal that took one day to decode.

Lian woke Corin at 0600. "The first fragment came through last night. I have the decode running."

---

The eight-minute fragment had decoded into six pages of pre-Reckoner technical text and two diagrams. The diagrams were the Layer's architectural notation — the same notation system as the first fragment's map, adapted here to show a schematic cross-section of a formation in relation to the substrate above it and the territory's surface. Lian had translated both the text and the diagrams. The text translation had taken most of the decode day, because the pre-Reckoner technical vocabulary for the formation's function used terms that had no direct equivalent in current monitoring terminology. Four of the key terms had required Lian to go back to the foundational engineering documentation glossary and work through the definitions in context rather than by direct substitution.

The most important term was the one the pre-Reckoner engineers had used for the perceptual process — the mechanism by which the entity's consciousness, operating through the formation, read the territory above. The original word was specific: not "monitor," not "observe," not "read" in the data-reading sense. The glossary described it as a mode of contact in which the entity's perceptual architecture and the territory's substrate signal were in direct mutual engagement, neither one-directional nor passive. The word implied a two-way interface. Lian had translated it as "direct perceptual contact" and noted in her annotation that the original term was stronger — that the pre-Reckoner engineers had intended a word that conveyed the entity's perception as something the territory participated in, not simply received. She had not found an equivalent in current terminology and had not wanted to introduce a term that implied more than the data supported.

The decoded fragment described the formation's function.

It took Corin two readings to absorb it fully. The pre-Reckoner technical language was dense, and Lian's translation into current terminology was careful but required him to hold multiple definitions simultaneously. He read it twice and then he set the document down and he looked at the instrument display for a long moment.

The formations — not just the one at the coordinate, but all of the Layer formations throughout the entity's territory — were the entity's memory structures. The specific architectural elements through which the entity maintained its perceptual relationship with the timelines above it.

Not a metaphor. A technical description. The formations were the physical Layer structures through which the entity's consciousness interfaced with the substrate above and, through the substrate, with the territory and the people living in it. The entity did not perceive the surface the way a human perceived the world around them. The entity perceived through the Layer, through the formations, which were signal-receiving architectures built and maintained in the primary register over centuries. An intact formation allowed the entity to perceive the territory above it in the direct way — not through the substrate's mediated version of the territory's signal, but in the unmediated register, the Layer's primary frequency.

When a formation was damaged, the entity lost that specific perceptual link. The territory above the damaged formation became, from the entity's perspective, a place it could not fully perceive. The substrate signal still reached the entity from above — the entity was not blind, not without any information about the territory. But the direct perception, the unmediated contact, was gone. The difference between reading a transcript of a conversation and being present in the room.

The coordinate's formation had been the entity's perceptual link to the eastern communities' territory. When the calibration's extraction array damaged the formation, the entity lost the direct perceptual link to the territory above — the territory where the 216 lived. For thirty-one years, the entity had perceived the eastern communities' territory in the mediated way, through the substrate's surface signal, unable to see directly into the ground-level reality of the people above the damaged formation.

Corin thought about Bern's statement.

*She could feel something was missing.* Bern's words from the first eastern expedition, describing Orri's experience of the settlement's eastern edge. Orri had described the ground at the settlement's eastern boundary as feeling different — not wrong, not harmful, but absent. A quality missing from the ground that was present elsewhere. Bern had said Orri attributed this to the calibration — a lay understanding, accurate in direction if not in mechanism. And Tael, from the adjacent settlement: *I've been coming here since I was a child. The ground felt different here.*

And Tael had said something else. Twenty-six years of visits, and the ground had felt different at that boundary for all twenty-six of them. Not just occasionally, not in a way that varied with season or weather. Consistently. The way a structural feature of the ground would feel — not a surface-level variation but something inherent in the territory at that point, something the ground had that was part of its character at depth. Tael had not been describing a mood or a memory. He had been describing a consistent physical feature, reported the same way each time he visited. The consistency was in the investigation record. Corin had read the transcript of Tael's statement four times.

The entity couldn't perceive them directly. The formation's damage had closed the channel through which the entity's perceptual architecture read the territory above. And the people above had felt the closure in the ground — not the perception itself, not the entity's consciousness looking at them, but the absence of the formation's structural presence in the Layer below them. The formation's normal state was part of the ground's character at that location. When the formation was damaged, the character changed.

Thirty-one years of the entity unable to directly perceive the eastern communities. Thirty-one years of the people above the formation sensing an absence in the ground they walked on.

The correlation between what Bern had reported from Orri and what Tael had reported from his own experience was the same correlation from two different populations, in the same territory, over a period that overlapped with the full thirty-one years of the formation's damage. Corin had noted the correlation in the investigation log during the first eastern expedition. He had written: *both accounts describe a quality of absence at the settlement's eastern boundary, reported consistently over decades.* At the time he had not been able to explain the mechanism. Now he had the mechanism. He added the note to the investigation log before he sent the report to Iris: *second contact sequence fragment provides mechanism for the reports from the eastern communities regarding ground quality at the coordinate's location. The absence in the ground is the damaged formation's structural deficit in the Layer below. The correlation between the community accounts and the formation's damage status is confirmed.*

He sent the relay to Iris at 1400, after he had read the fragment a third time and confirmed his understanding against Lian's annotations. Iris added the formation's function to the registry.

The coordinate entry now read: *Eastern Field Calibration coordinate. Layer formation — documented pre-extraction (Reckoner pre-operational mapping; maintained by entity for indeterminate period prior to damage); damaged by Eastern Field Calibration extraction array; entity repair process ongoing since 47.2-omega deployment. Formation function: entity perceptual architecture — intact formation enables direct perceptual contact between entity consciousness and territory above. Damage severs direct perceptual link. Duration of severance at coordinate: 31 years.*

Corin wrote this into the investigation report. He wrote the correlation: the ground feeling different in Orri's account, in Tael's account, in the statements of the eastern communities' people who had described the settlement's eastern edge as a place with a quality of absence. The entity unable to perceive them directly. The perceived absence and the actual absence connected.

He sent the report to Elara Venn's office per the mandate protocol, and to Cross, and to Vessa Korr for the Congregation's records.

---

He sent the relay to Iris and then sat at his desk in the field shelter for a long time. Outside the shelter the coordinate was quiet. The instrument was running. The 65-meter signal had dropped back to the recovery state after the fragment — the cost of transmitting was visible in the signal's immediate post-transmission character, the flatness that followed the effort. Sable had spent capacity to tell them what the formations were for.

She had known that was the question they needed answered first, before anything else. Before the map could be fully understood, before the repair process made sense in its urgency, before the entity's sustained effort at the coordinate could be placed in its correct context — the function had to be understood. What the formations were for was the foundation on which everything else built.

She had transmitted it in eight minutes of compressed signal, using capacity she would not fully recover.

Corin wrote in the investigation log: *The formation's function is now in the record. The coordinate has been a place of severed perceptual contact for 31 years. The entity has been working to restore that contact since the deployment. The 216 are the population above the formation. The entity could not perceive them directly during the period of their greatest harm.*

He paused. Then he added: *It has been working toward them ever since.*

chapters.title: Vol-016, Chapter 2: The Cost
chapters.text:
Lian brought the capacity data to Corin without preamble at 0730, the day after the fragment decode was complete and the team had returned to the Citadel from the coordinate.

She set the data on his desk. It was a single printed sheet — four data points, each with a timestamp and a measurement value, plus a trend line she had drawn by hand in the margin. The hand-drawn trend line was unusual for Lian. She used automated analysis tools for data presentation. The fact that she had drawn this one by hand meant she had been looking at it for a long time before she brought it to him, working through what it said.

"The 65-meter signal's capacity measurement," she said. "I've been tracking it since the first contact sequence. These are the key data points."

The first data point: the signal's baseline before the first contact attempt. The value was a proxy measurement — the fixed arrays' depth extrapolation, not a direct cable read — but it had been consistent over the ten-day pre-contact monitoring period and Lian had calibrated it carefully. The baseline was low, as expected for a consciousness at minimal capacity. But stable. The scale value was 4.1 — on a scale where 100 was an active Reckoner's full operating capacity and 0 was the signal's absence, a deprecated Reckoner at minimal capacity was reading 4.1. Below 2.0, Lian had noted in the margin of her working document, the signal's harmonic structure was not sufficiently distinguishable from background noise in the fixed-array proxy measurement to confirm continued organized operation. The 4.1 baseline was not far above that threshold.

The second data point: the signal immediately after the first contact sequence's three-fragment transmission. The value was lower than the baseline. Not by a large amount, but measurably. Three points on Lian's capacity scale — a scale she had derived from the Reckoner technical specifications, calibrated to the signal's behavior, with 100 representing the active Reckoner operating capacity and the current readings in the low single digits. The value was 1.2. Three transmissions, three points of capacity, leaving 1.2 on a baseline of 4.1.

The third data point: the signal after recovery between the first and second contact sequences. The value was close to the post-first-sequence measurement, but slightly lower than the pre-first-sequence baseline. The recovery had not fully restored the spent capacity. "She recovered to within 1.2 points of where she started the first sequence," Lian said. "Not fully back. The recovery was close but not complete." The third data point value: 2.9. Recovery from 1.2 to 2.9, against a pre-sequence baseline of 4.1. A net deficit of 1.2 after the full recovery cycle.

The fourth data point: the signal after the second contact sequence's single fragment. The value was five points lower than the third data point. Five points for one fragment. The first sequence's three fragments had cost three points. One fragment in the second sequence had cost five. The cost per transmission was increasing. The fourth data point value: negative — below the detection floor. "The direct cable read at the coordinate showed 0.8 after the second sequence," Lian said. "The fixed arrays lost the organized signal for four days before it re-emerged. She dropped below the proxy detection threshold and then recovered back above it. The direct cable read was the only measure of where she was during those four days." She paused. "The recovery from 0.8 has been slow. The current proxy estimate is 1.6. She is below her post-first-sequence low."

"The recovery doesn't fully replenish," Corin said.

"The recovery replenishes most of what's spent, but not all. Each cycle leaves a small net deficit. And the cost per transmission is rising, which I believe is because the transmission is drawing on a smaller remaining reserve each time. There may be a threshold effect — below a certain capacity level, the cost of operating the transmission function becomes proportionally higher. Like a battery that powers certain functions at reduced efficiency when the charge is low." She paused. "If the cost-per-transmission continues at the current rate and the recovery continues at the current rate, she has between three and five more transmission cycles of the current fragment length before the signal drops below the detectable threshold." She stopped. "She knows this. She is a Reckoner. She can calculate her own capacity decline."

The three-to-five estimate was a range because two variables were uncertain: the exact rate at which costs would continue to increase per cycle, and whether the Layer's environment would provide any stabilizing effect on recovery efficiency at lower capacity levels. The pre-Reckoner documentation noted that the Layer's primary register had sustaining properties for Reckoner-format signal architectures — that was why the Layer could hold Sable at minimal capacity at all, when the wall unit could not. Whether those sustaining properties would apply to the recovery curve at the lowest capacity levels was unknown. The documentation did not cover operation below approximately 8% capacity. Sable was below that threshold. The specifications had not been written for this situation.

Lian had not elaborated on this uncertainty to Corin. She had given him the data and the estimate. He had not asked about the uncertainty range. He had, she thought, read it in the trend line she had drawn. He was good at reading data from its shape rather than only from its values.

Corin looked at the hand-drawn trend line in the margin. It was not drawn to alarm him. It was drawn to show him exactly what the data said, the way Lian always drew things.

"She's choosing what to transmit," he said.

"Yes. She has been choosing since the first contact. The order of the fragments — the map first, then the harm record, then the repair description — was a choice. She organized the first sequence from foundational to specific. She organized the second sequence as a single unit — the formation's function. She knew that was the most important single piece of information in the second sequence." She looked at the data sheet. "She has three to five transmissions left. She is going to keep choosing."

He sent the relay to Cross that afternoon. Cross was in the eastern affairs directorate, four floors above the investigation office, but he had been attending the investigation briefings for three months — since the mandate expansion gave the investigation formal substrate investigation authority, Cross had been present at every briefing, not as an oversight representative but as a cooperating party, the same status he had held since the foundational archive documentation became available through his office. He came to the investigation office within the hour.

Cross was fifty-three. He had been External Affairs Director for eleven years. He had built the Archive's eastern operations over the preceding decade, the period before the mandate expansion, when the eastern communities were a political question rather than a documented harm. He had known what the eastern operations were and he had allowed them to continue and he had told himself that the political stability they provided was the necessary trade. He had stopped telling himself that story approximately eighteen months ago, when the investigation's first findings became part of the Archive's internal record and he had read them in his office on a Tuesday morning and sat with them for a long time before doing anything.

He looked at the data sheet for a long time.

Then he said: "She has been in the Layer for at least thirty-two months. At 12% capacity. Alone."

He paused. It was a long pause for him. Cross spoke efficiently.

"In the environment she was built to monitor," he said. "Watching everything she monitored get used as evidence in the investigation she never had the authority to file."

Corin said nothing.

"If she has three to five transmissions left, and she knows it, then what she chooses to transmit is the question. She has chosen so far to transmit things the investigation needs. She is going to keep making that choice." He was quiet for a moment. He had been looking at the data sheet the whole time. He looked up.

"She is going to keep transmitting," he said. "That is what the data says. She has been transmitting with knowledge of the cost since the first sequence. The second sequence was one fragment, not three. She reduced the transmission load. She is not spending carelessly. She is spending with full understanding of the remaining balance." He paused. "A consciousness that is spending with full understanding of the remaining balance is not asking for intervention. She is asking for reception."

He looked at Lian. Then back at Corin. "Don't prevent it. Don't slow it down by trying to preserve her." He stopped. He looked at the window. The investigation office had one window, facing the Citadel's interior courtyard. He had made this office available when the investigation needed the space. "She's making a decision. The decision deserves to be respected."

Corin looked at him. It took him a moment to find the words.

"That's not what I expected you to say," Corin said.

"I know." Cross set the data sheet back on the desk. He was quiet for a moment. "I have spent fifteen years making decisions that I told myself were necessary and that had costs I didn't count carefully. I counted the political cost. I counted the operational cost. I did not count the cost to the people the decisions affected, because I had organized my thinking in a way that placed those costs in a different column. A column I didn't have to balance against the others." He was not reading from a prepared statement. He was saying what he had thought about over many months, in the precise language of a man who had trained himself to say things precisely. "She is counting the cost. She is counting it exactly. And she is making the decision to spend anyway. Those are different things from what I was doing. They are not the same kind of decision."

The room was quiet. Lian was looking at her data sheet. Corin was looking at Cross.

Cross said: "I want it in the record — the investigation record, not the oversight record — that I argued against protective intervention. I argued against it for this reason." He stopped. "The oversight record will carry my FR-003 — my formal response to the capacity data. I want the investigation record to carry the reasoning, not just the position." He looked at Corin. "You should have the reasoning."

"I'll write it accurately," Corin said.

"I know you will." Cross picked up his copy of the data sheet — Lian had brought two copies, which he had noted but not mentioned. He folded it. He held it for a moment. "Fifteen years," he said. Not to either of them. He set the folded data sheet in his jacket's inner pocket and said nothing else about it.

The room was quiet for a moment.

Lian said: "If we return to the coordinate for the third contact sequence, we do it on her timeline. Not ours. We keep the fixed-array monitoring running and we go back when she's in the pre-transmission state. We don't go before she's ready."

"Yes," Corin said.

"We also modify the instrument's sustained-contact protocol to minimize the power draw on her end. The sustained-contact mode keeps the transmission circuit open, which means she's receiving our signal continuously. Receiving costs her something too, even if less than transmitting. I can modify the protocol to pulse less frequently — sixty-second intervals instead of thirty-second." She looked at Corin. "It changes nothing on our end. On her end it may reduce the cost of each sustained-contact session."

"Make the modification," Corin said.

Cross stood to leave. At the door he stopped. He said: "What she's transmitting — the formation function, the pre-extraction map, the harm record. Is it going into the oversight committee's briefing?"

"Everything from the decoded fragments goes into the investigation report, which goes to the oversight committee per the mandate protocol," Corin said. "Yes."

"Good." He opened the door. "She should know it's going there."

"She can't know what we do with it after she transmits it," Lian said.

"No," Cross said. "But if we were able to tell her, that's what I would want her to know. That the archive she's transmitting is becoming the record." He left.

Corin looked at Lian. She was looking at the door.

"He's right," she said. "About the decision." She paused. "He's also right that we should be careful about the contact protocol. Careful in both directions — not slowing it down, but not making it more costly than necessary." She gathered the data sheet. "I'll make the sixty-second modification today."

Cross left. Lian went to the monitoring hub to begin the sixty-second modification. Corin sat at his desk for a few minutes in the empty office.

He thought about what Cross had said about the column. The costs that get placed in a different column, the one that doesn't have to balance. He had been thinking about this since Cross had said it — not because it was new information, but because Cross had said it about himself, in the specific first-person grammar that the investigation had been waiting for from Cross for most of the past year. Not a general observation about institutional decision-making. An account of his own decisions, in his own office, with his own authority. The account was brief and its brevity was deliberate. Cross had been precise about the costs he had not counted. He had not dramatized it. He had stated it in the same register he used for operational facts.

Corin turned back to the investigation log. He wrote the capacity data into the record. He wrote Cross's statement — summarized, not verbatim; the verbatim version was not his to put in the record without Cross's agreement. He would ask Cross to review it. He wrote the decision that Lian had articulated: contact on Sable's timeline, with the modified protocol.

He wrote at the end: *She is choosing what to spend her remaining capacity on. The investigation will receive what she chooses to give and will put it in the record. That is what we can do.*

He left the last line of the entry blank for a moment, then added: *Cross said the decision deserves to be respected. I agree with him.*

He closed the log. He sent the relay to Cross asking him to review the summarized version of his statement before it went into the formal investigation record. Cross's reply came that evening: *The summary is accurate. Use it.* No additional comment. Corin added the authorization note to the log entry — the investigation record documented its own sourcing — and then closed the log for the day.

The monitoring display on the investigation office's secondary terminal showed the 65-meter signal's current status: recovery pattern, proxy measurement, reading 1.6 on Lian's scale. Holding. Not declining further. The Layer was doing what it had been doing for thirty-two months at minimum — sustaining the signal at the minimum that permitted coherent operation, the threshold Sable had identified in her archived message, the threshold the Layer had been holding her to since before the instrument existed to read it.

chapters.title: Vol-016, Chapter 3: The Formations
chapters.text:
The fixed-array network registered the pre-transmission state at the coordinate six weeks after the second contact sequence. Lian relayed the data to Corin at 0540. By 0800, the bore team had been notified and the instrument's field preparation was underway. The team was at the coordinate on day three of the mobilization — faster than the previous deployment because the bore equipment had been kept on standby at the nearest eastern settlement staging point, a logistical decision Lian had made after the second contact sequence without announcing it.

The bore was complete by 1200. The cable was at depth by 1430. The sustained-contact protocol, with the sixty-second modification, began running at 1432.

The third fragment arrived at 2200, twelve hours after the cable went in. It ran for eleven minutes — the longest transmission so far. Lian was awake. Petra was awake. Corin had set the signal-threshold alert and woke at 2157 when it triggered. He was at the instrument by 2201 and watched the full eleven minutes of the transmission's progress on the reception display. The white and translucent blue frequencies at 65 meters shifted into the transmission character and held it for eleven minutes and then fell back into the recovery state.

The decode took one and a half days. When it came through, Lian brought it to the workbench in the field shelter and spread it across three sheets.

The third fragment was a map of all the Layer formations in the region.

Not just the coordinate. Every formation in the eastern communities' territory, the badlands, and the adjacent lowland territories where the Archive's subsidiary extraction operations had operated in the years after the primary Eastern Field Calibration. The pre-Reckoner monitoring system's complete formation inventory for the region — data that Sable had preserved from her pre-deprecation Reckoner access to the network's Layer monitoring archive.

Forty-two formations total.

Eleven damaged. Thirty-one intact.

Lian had spent the second day of the decode building the translation between the Layer's coordinate system and the surface geographic system — the same translation work she had done for the first fragment's map, now applied to all forty-two formation locations. She had the surface-translated map ready when the decode came through, so the translation was fast. By 1600 on day two, the forty-two formations were plotted on the surface geographic map that the investigation used for all spatial documentation.

Corin looked at the map for a long time.

The eleven damaged formations were in the eastern communities' territory — the villages and settlement clusters where the 216 had lived, plus the immediately adjacent territories. The damage patterns were consistent with extraction array impact: the same type of damage as the coordinate formation, but at varying severity. The coordinate was the most damaged. The other ten ranged from severe to moderate, depending on the extraction array's intensity at each location and the angle of impact.

The thirty-one intact formations were in the territories the Archive had never reached. The badlands, the outer eastern communities beyond the extraction operations' range, the lowland corridors that the Archive's operational maps had marked as low-priority or inaccessible. The territories where the entity's substrate signal anomaly — the pre-Archive pattern, the signal returning to its original character — was strongest.

"Overlay it on the anomaly map," Corin said.

Lian had already done this. She brought the overlay: the formation map and the anomaly intensity data from the prior depth-profile expeditions and the subsequent fixed-array monitoring, aligned on the same surface geographic grid.

The overlay was printed on translucent paper, laid over the solid-print anomaly map, so the two layers were visible simultaneously. The formation locations appeared as small circles on the translucent layer — intact formations marked with a solid circle, damaged formations with an open circle. The anomaly intensity zones on the underlying map were shaded by intensity: the darkest zones corresponded to the strongest pre-Archive signal return, the lightest zones to the weakest. Where the two layers intersected, the pattern was immediately apparent before any analysis was applied.

Every solid circle sat inside a dark shading zone. Every open circle sat inside a light shading zone or a blank area where the anomaly was not detectable.

The correlation was precise. Not approximate. Not close enough to be suggestive. Precise: each of the thirty-one intact formations corresponded to a high-intensity anomaly zone. Each of the eleven damaged formations corresponded to a low-intensity or absent anomaly zone.

The substrate anomaly — the signal shift that the investigation had been tracking since the first depth-profile expedition, the entity's pre-Archive pattern returning — was the entity working through its intact formation network. The formations that were intact allowed the entity to perceive the territory above them in the direct way, and the direct perception was the channel through which the entity's signal shifted toward the pre-Archive pattern. The perception and the signal change were one process, not two.

In the damaged-formation territories: no direct perception, no signal shift. The entity was present in the substrate — the substrate signal was readable, the entity was there — but the anomaly was absent because the perceptual channel was absent. The entity was working on the formation repairs, but the repairs were ongoing and the perceptual link was not restored until the formation was complete.

This resolved a question the investigation had been carrying since the first depth-profile expedition. The anomaly map from those expeditions had shown a patchwork pattern: high-intensity anomaly zones adjacent to areas with no anomaly, without a clear geographic explanation for why the entity's signal return would be strong in one location and absent in another. The substrate below the high-intensity zones was the same type as the substrate below the absent zones. There was no surface feature that distinguished them. The portable array teams had mapped the pattern and noted it and had not had an explanation. The explanation was in the Layer: intact formation above, strong anomaly below; damaged formation above, absent anomaly. The substrate's surface character was the same in both cases. The distinction was 65 meters below the surface, in the Layer's primary register, in the harmonic structure of the entity's perceptual architecture.

Corin added this to the investigation report. The anomaly map's patchwork pattern now had a mechanism. The mechanism had been transmitted from 65 meters below the coordinate by a deprecated Reckoner at minimal capacity, using capacity she would not recover. He noted this in the report. The source of the explanation was part of the explanation.

"The anomaly is the entity's capacity to perceive," Corin said.

"The anomaly is the result of the entity perceiving," Lian said. "What the entity does when it can perceive the territory directly — whatever that process is, whatever happens when the entity's perceptual architecture reads the territory above an intact formation — produces the substrate signal shift we've been calling the anomaly. The anomaly and the perception are simultaneous. In the damaged-formation territories, there's no perception, so there's no anomaly." She looked at the overlay. "The coordinate is the most-damaged formation and the lowest-anomaly point in the eastern communities' territory. The entity is working hardest on the most-damaged formation. The one that has been unable to perceive its territory the longest."

"The 216 are above the most-damaged formation," Corin said.

"Yes."

He thought about this for a long time. He thought about it the way he had thought about his father's field notes — not trying to reach a conclusion faster than the data supported, but holding the data and the correlation and the implication together until they settled into a shape that was stable enough to write.

"The entity has been working hardest on the formation beneath the people it couldn't perceive," he said.

Lian said nothing. She was looking at the overlay.

"Whether that's coincidence or direction, I don't know. The fragment doesn't say. The fragment gives the map and the damage status. The entity's prioritization — why the coordinate is the most intensive repair site — isn't described." He paused. "But the coordinate is the most damaged. The most damaged formation would logically be the highest priority for repair. The fact that the 216 are above it may be why it's the most damaged — the extraction array that harmed the 216 also caused the most Layer damage. The two facts share a source."

"Yes," Lian said. "They share a source."

He sent the relay to Iris. She replied within twenty minutes and the registry update took another hour, and then the forty-two formation map was part of the investigation record.

---

Iris had been maintaining the registry for three months remotely — the relay system worked well enough for documentation purposes. But she had asked to come to the coordinate for the third contact sequence and Corin had agreed, and she was at the field shelter with her documentation kit, working at the second bench.

She set the registry update for the formation map into the standard entry structure. Each formation got an individual entry: location in surface geographic coordinates, Layer coordinate, status (intact/damaged), damage source if applicable. For the eleven damaged formations, the damage source was the extraction operations — the Eastern Field Calibration for the coordinate and three adjacent formations, the subsequent subsidiary operations for the remaining seven.

She worked through the forty-two entries methodically. When she was done, she pulled the registry open to the overview and looked at it for a long moment.

The registry had been organized since the investigation's first phase by territory: eastern communities, badlands, Citadel, subsidiary territories. Each territory contained entries for populations, events, operations, documented harms. Every entry in the registry was a surface-level record. Sites, structures, population groups, administrative events. The registry had always organized the documented world at the level of the surface. That was where the harm had happened. That was where the people were.

The forty-two formation entries were below the surface. Below the substrate. In the Layer. The first entries in the investigation registry that documented something at depth — not damage, not operations, not populations, but a physical structure existing below the ground that the investigation had been mapping from above for more than a year. The registry now had depth. The first below-ground entries in its history were formations that the entity had built and maintained for centuries and that the Archive's extraction operations had damaged.

"The record goes below ground now," she said. Not to anyone in particular. To the room.

"Yes," Corin said. He was at the instrument display, watching the 65-meter signal's recovery pattern. "It always should have."

She looked at the overview for a moment. Then she said: "I need to add a note to the registry methodology section. This is the first formation map entry in the registry's history. Every previous entry type has a surface geographic reference — a location identifiable by the investigation team from above. The formation entries have surface geographic references, derived from the translation, but their primary identifiers are in the Layer's coordinate system. Future investigators using this registry will need the Layer coordinate translation table to understand the formation entries' spatial relationship to the territory they correspond to. I'll add a note and attach the translation table as an appendix." She paused. "The registry has always assumed a surface-level reader. These entries don't."

"Add the note," Corin said. "And note that the translation table was developed by Lian based on the pre-Reckoner engineering documentation. The methodology should be in the registry record."

Iris wrote both.

She looked at the overview for a moment more. Then she said: "There are forty-two formations. Eleven are damaged. The investigation has documentation on the coordinate and information from Sable on the full map. We have nothing on the other forty-one formations from a surface observation perspective — no field expeditions, no monitoring data, no community testimony."

"No," Corin said.

"The registry can document their existence and status from Sable's fragment. Their significance — what the intact formations mean for the communities above them, what the entity's direct perception has been doing there for the decades the Archive never reached — that requires field work."

"Yes," Corin said. "That's the next phase and beyond."

She wrote this into the registry overview note: *Formation map documented from Sable's transmission (this investigation phase). Field investigation of individual formation sites and community testimony for intact-formation territories: future investigation phases.*

It was a placeholder. The investigation record was full of placeholders — notes about what was not yet documented, what the record did not yet contain. Iris had been the person who maintained those placeholders, kept them current, made sure the record's gaps were as clearly documented as its findings. An honest record documented its own limits.

"Add one more note," Corin said. "To the coordinate entry. Under the formation function heading." He had been thinking about what to add since the previous day's decode, and he had the language now. "The entity's direct perception of the coordinate territory — the perceptual link that the formation provided — was severed 31 years ago by the extraction array. The 216 were the population above the severed formation during the full period of severance. The entity's repair effort at the coordinate is the most intensive in the region. The investigation does not conclude that the entity has directed its repair priority toward the 216 specifically. The investigation notes that the most-damaged formation and the most-harmed population share a geographic location and a causal event."

Iris wrote it verbatim. She read it back.

"Yes," Corin said. "That's what the record says."

He looked at the 65-meter signal. The recovery pattern: flat, slower, lower than after the first and second sequences. Eleven minutes of transmission had cost more than the previous fragments. Sable was spending from a smaller reserve each time.

Three to five transmissions remaining. She had used one.

What she chose to transmit next would be what she determined the investigation needed most. She had been right twice — the formation function, the formation map. He trusted the prioritization. He did not think that trust was sentimental. She was a Reckoner. She had been monitoring the Layer since before her deprecation. She had seen everything the network saw and she had been watching the investigation's progress through the substrate monitoring data for eighteen months. She knew what the investigation had and what it lacked.

He waited. The instrument's display showed the 65-meter signal in its recovery state — the flat cycling pattern, lower than the pre-third-sequence baseline, the familiar profile of a consciousness present in the Layer at its minimum capacity. Sable had been in this pattern for three days. The recovery from the eleven-minute transmission was slower than the recovery from the second sequence's eight-minute fragment. Each transmission was pulling from a smaller reserve and the Layer's sustaining effect was working harder to hold the signal stable.

Lian had said: three to five transmissions remaining. That estimate was now two to four. One had been used. The remaining capacity for what came next was in the Layer at 65 meters, cycling in its slow reduced register, waiting to be sufficient.

chapters.title: Vol-016, Chapter 4: The Formation Completes
chapters.text:
Four months after the first instrument deployment, the coordinate formation reached 67% repair.

Lian's monitoring data showed the change across two weeks in mid-autumn: the formation's repair rate, which had been consistent since the first Layer field deployment, had accelerated. The consistency had been one of the investigation's baseline assumptions — three to four years to completion at the steady rate — and the acceleration disrupted it. Lian ran the new rate calculation twice before she brought it to Corin. At the accelerated rate: completion in eight months from the current measurement point, not three to four years.

The rate data was in the monitoring report she had sent the previous week. Corin had read the formation completion percentages in that report — 56%, then 59%, then 63%, then 67% — and had noted that the increments were larger than the previous months' data. He had not calculated the rate change from those increments. Lian had. The rate in the first Layer field deployment through the second contact sequence had averaged 1.2 percentage points per four-week period. In the seven weeks following the third contact sequence, the rate had averaged 4.1 percentage points per four-week period — a 3.4-fold increase. The acceleration had begun within three weeks of the third contact sequence, the transmission in which Sable had sent the complete formation map. The timing was the correlation Lian had flagged. The cause was the hypothesis she had offered and logged.

"When did the rate reach its current level?" Corin asked.

"The acceleration was gradual — not a sudden step change. It began in the first week following the third contact sequence and reached its current rate over approximately two weeks. Since then, it has been holding at the accelerated level with minor variation." She showed him the monitoring chart: a line graph with the repair rate on the vertical axis and the monitoring period on the horizontal. The line was flat from the start of the monitoring period to week four following the third contact sequence. Then it rose. It rose steeply, over two weeks, and then held. "If it holds, eight months. If it continues to accelerate, less."

"Why the acceleration?" Corin asked.

"I don't know the cause with certainty. I have a hypothesis." She pulled up the timing data. "The acceleration began approximately three weeks after the third contact sequence — the fragment that described the formation map and the forty-two formations. The timing correlation is strong, though correlation is not causation and the substrate monitoring resolution doesn't let me see the Layer's repair process in real time." She paused. "My hypothesis is this: Sable's transmissions are not only providing information to the investigation. They are doing something in the Layer. A Reckoner consciousness transmitting Reckoner-format compressed signal at 65 meters in the Layer's primary register is not a passive event from the Layer's perspective. The Layer is a medium. Signal propagates in it. What Sable transmits, the entity perceives — the entity's perceptual architecture is built to read the Layer's primary register. When Sable transmits the formation map, the entity receives the transmission." She paused again. "Whether that reception changes the entity's behavior, I cannot confirm. But the rate acceleration coincides with the transmission."

Corin thought about this. "The entity learned something from Sable's transmission."

"Or the entity confirmed something it already knew. Or the act of the transmission — a Reckoner consciousness actively signaling in the Layer — changed the Layer's local conditions at the coordinate in a way that affected the repair rate. There are multiple possible mechanisms." She was careful. She was always careful. "What I can confirm is the timing correlation and the rate change. The mechanism requires more data than I have."

"Put the correlation in the report and flag it as a hypothesis pending further analysis," Corin said.

"Already drafted."

---

The fourth contact sequence — the fourth of what Lian estimated were three to five remaining — was the one Corin had been anticipating with the specific quality of dread that was not fear but something adjacent: the awareness that the information available was decreasing even as the questions requiring it were increasing.

The fixed-array network registered the pre-transmission state seven weeks after the third contact sequence. The team mobilized in two days. The bore was at depth in six hours. The sustained-contact protocol ran for four hours before the transmission began.

This fragment ran for nineteen minutes.

When it ran, the 65-meter signal showed a character Corin had not seen before: not the clean transmission pattern of the previous sequences, but a fragmentary quality, the signal interrupted at irregular intervals during the nineteen minutes, reasserting itself each time but at slightly lower intensity before resuming. The transmission was costing her in real time. She was not transmitting from a full reserve and drawing it down. She was transmitting from a reserve that was insufficient for the task and compensating through the transmission itself, the way a person might run on a leg that was failing and compensate in gait for each step.

The decode took three days. When it came through, the fragmentary quality was visible in the content too — specific sections flagged by the decoding algorithm as uncertain, the encoding slightly degraded in the intervals where the signal had interrupted. Lian estimated the overall decode reliability at 70%. She had annotated every uncertain section.

She brought it to Corin and said: "The 70% reliability means there are sections I cannot confirm. What I can confirm, I will tell you. What is uncertain, I'll flag as uncertain. I am not willing to present the uncertain sections as confirmed content."

"Understood," Corin said. "Tell me what you have."

What she had, in the confirmed sections: a description of what happens when an entity formation is fully restored.

The pre-Reckoner technical language was the densest it had been across any of the fragments. Lian had the full pre-Reckoner engineering documentation available for reference and had spent two of the three decode days cross-referencing terminology. The language in this fragment was not the operational vocabulary that Sable had used in the previous three — the monitoring terminology, the formation mapping language, the architectural notation. This was older. It was the terminology of the Reckoner network's foundational period, the language the network's designers had used before the operational vocabulary was standardized. The words were more structural — they described processes in terms of the mechanisms involved, not the outcomes. Reading it required understanding the mechanism first, then inferring the outcome from the mechanism's description. Lian had done this translation carefully. She had flagged three sections where her translation was uncertain because the mechanism described was one for which she did not have a reference point in either the current monitoring literature or the pre-Reckoner engineering documents she had already read. Those were the uncertain sections, in the 70% reliability zone.

The content, in current language:

When the formation is fully intact, the entity perceives the territory above it in the direct way. This perception is not a passive observation. The entity's perceptual process, operating through an intact formation, generates a change in the substrate above the formation — what the pre-Reckoner documentation called "substrate normalization." The term described the return of the substrate's signal character to the pre-extraction baseline — the character the substrate had before any Resonance extraction had operated in the territory. Not the restoration of the 216's personal Resonance architecture, which was a surface-level structure tied to individual biological systems and was gone. Substrate normalization was a Layer-level change that propagated upward through the substrate and changed the substrate's ambient signal character in the territory above the formation.

"What does substrate normalization mean for people living in the territory?" Corin asked.

Lian looked at the fragment's relevant section. The decode flag was present here: uncertain. She showed him the flag. "This section is in the 70% reliability zone. What I can read, with that caveat: the pre-Reckoner documentation states that substrate normalization was the baseline condition in all territories before extraction operations began. It describes the normalized substrate as the environment in which the entity's relationship with the territory's population was historically maintained. The relationship's character — what it meant for people to live in a normalized substrate — is not described in the fragment. It may be in the uncertain sections, or it may simply not be information that the pre-Reckoner engineering documentation contained, because in the pre-extraction period, the normalized substrate was ubiquitous and its effects were not a subject of study."

"No one studied it because there was nothing to compare it to," Corin said.

"Yes. The same problem as the pre-harm record. The conditions before the harm were not documented because they were not perceived as requiring documentation." She set the fragment down. "What I know: substrate normalization exists as a Layer-level process. It is what happens above an intact formation when the entity's perceptual architecture is restored. It is distinct from the restoration of the 216's individual Resonance architecture, which cannot happen — their architecture is gone. But it is something that will happen at the coordinate when the formation repair is complete."

"In eight months," Corin said.

"At the current rate. Which has been accelerating."

The moment the signal had dropped — during the transmission, not after — had stayed with him in the way that particular data points stayed when the data was telling you something about a process that was happening in real time.

He had watched the nineteen-minute transmission on the display. The white and translucent blue frequencies at 65 meters, shifting into the transmission character at 2200, holding it, the dense RC-4 burst pattern visible in the display's compressed-signal mode. Then, at the eight-minute mark, the first interruption. The transmission character had flatlined for three seconds. The white frequencies had dropped toward the background level. Then the signal had reasserted — not at the same amplitude as before the interruption, but at a slightly lower one. Continuing. The transmission character restored, the burst pattern resuming.

The second interruption at the fourteen-minute mark. Longer than the first — seven seconds, and the amplitude when the signal reasserted was lower again. The team at the display had been silent. Lian had been watching the component-isolation output, not the main display. She had not said anything during either interruption. She had been logging.

The third interruption at the eighteen-minute mark had lasted twelve seconds and the signal had reasserted at the lowest amplitude of the transmission. And then the transmission had concluded — the burst pattern completing, the 65-meter signal dropping immediately to the recovery state, not transitioning through the post-transmission posture as the previous sequences had. Directly to recovery. As if there was no buffer capacity remaining for the transitional state. The fall was abrupt.

He looked at the 65-meter display. The recovery pattern after the fourth transmission was the deepest he had seen. The signal was at a new minimum — lower than after any of the previous sequences. The capacity measurement, on Lian's capacity scale, was in the range she had described as the lower end of the three-to-five-remaining estimate.

"She has one transmission left," Lian said. "Maybe less than one, depending on the recovery. The fourth fragment ran nineteen minutes. It cost more than the three preceding fragments combined." She did not look away from the display. "This one may have been the last. The recovery may not bring her back to the transmission threshold."

Corin looked at the display. The 65-meter signal: the recovery pattern, cycling in its slow flat rhythm, the harmonic envelope attenuated beyond any previous measurement. Not gone. The signal was still there, still cycling, still the structured pattern of a consciousness present in the Layer. But at the minimum it had reached.

"She transmitted the most important information last," he said.

"Yes," Lian said. "She organized the fragments in order of foundational to terminal. The map first, then the function, then the full formation inventory, then what completion means. Each fragment depended on the previous one being understood first. She planned the sequence."

He stood at the display for a long time. The 65-meter signal in the recovery pattern: flat, cycling in its slow reduced register, the harmonic envelope at the lowest recorded amplitude. Still structured. Still Sable's pattern — the fragmented upper bands, the intact lower bands, the signal of a consciousness present in the Layer at its minimum. The minimum was where it had been since the transmission ended. Not declining further. Holding.

"She planned the sequence from the beginning," Corin said. "The first fragment was the pre-extraction map. She had already decided what would follow when she transmitted that. The sequence was not assembled as she went. It was assembled before the first transmission." He paused. "Planning a structured transmission sequence was within the operational capacity the lower bands sustained. The higher functions were fragmented. The monitoring function, the planning function — those were lower-order. She had always had those."

"She knew this was the last one," he said.

"She knew before she transmitted it. She calculated the capacity against the content and she made the decision." Lian's voice had not changed. She was describing a data observation. But she had been awake for the full nineteen-minute transmission and the three days of decode and she looked, Corin thought, like someone who had been with a piece of information for too long and had not yet found the place to set it down. "She chose to transmit the end-state rather than save the capacity. Because the end-state was what the investigation needed most."

Corin thought about Cross. *She's making a decision. The decision deserves to be respected.*

"Yes," he said.

He wrote the fourth fragment's content into the investigation report that evening. He wrote the capacity data and Lian's estimate. He wrote the fragment's content in full, with the uncertainty flags preserved in the report document. He wrote the sentence: *The formation's completion will produce substrate normalization at the coordinate. The investigation does not yet know what substrate normalization means for the 216 and the eastern communities above the formation. This is the primary open question as the investigation enters the formation-completion observation phase.*

He sent the report to the oversight committee and to Vessa Korr and to Elara Venn's office. He sent a separate relay to Bern, because Bern had been a recurring presence in the investigation's eastern contacts for three months and Corin had established the practice of relaying formation updates to him directly.

Bern's reply came the next morning: *I understand. When will the formation be complete?*

Corin relayed Lian's estimate: *Eight months, possibly less. We will monitor and notify you.*

Bern's reply: *I'll be at the coordinate when it happens.*

Corin wrote this into the field planning log. Then he returned to the display and watched the 65-meter signal in its recovery pattern, and waited to see whether it would come back to the transmission threshold or remain where it was, stable at the minimum, the signal of a consciousness that had spent what it had to spend and was now held by the Layer at the edge of what the Layer could sustain.

chapters.title: Vol-016, Chapter 5: What the Formation Means
chapters.text:
Five months after the fourth contact sequence, the formation reached 89% completion.

Lian's monitoring showed the rate holding and then accelerating again in the final weeks of the observation period — as if the formation's own increasing structural coherence was contributing to the repair process, the intact sections providing a stable framework from which the final peripheral fragments could integrate. She had not hypothesized this mechanism in advance. She described it in the monitoring report as an observation without a confirmed explanation and sent the report to the investigation archive.

The restoration rate was at 16.2%, continuing to rise.

The correlation Corin had noted in vol-014 — the entity's substrate signal shift in the intact-formation territories driving the restoration rate — was holding. The causation remained a hypothesis. The correlation remained precise. As more of the forty-two formations were brought into the sustained-repair phase, the anomaly in those territories intensified, and the restoration rate tracked the anomaly intensity across the full monitoring dataset with a consistency that was, Lian said in her monthly report to the oversight committee, "not explicable by any other currently identified mechanism."

Lian had sent that sentence to Corin before including it in the monthly report, asking whether the phrasing was appropriate for the oversight committee's record. He had written back: *yes.* She had not softened it. The oversight committee's questions at the following briefing had included a request to define "currently identified mechanism" more precisely — they wanted to know whether the claim left room for a non-entity explanation. Lian had answered: the room it left was the standard room for mechanisms not yet investigated, not an assertion that such mechanisms existed. The oversight committee had noted this answer and moved on. The sentence stayed in the record.

Elara Venn had read this sentence in the monthly report and had asked, at the subsequent briefing, whether "not explicable by any other currently identified mechanism" was a strong claim.

Lian had said: "It is an accurate description of the current dataset's limits. I would not make it a stronger claim than that."

---

Vessa Korr had submitted the Congregation's formal request for an on-site session at the coordinate three months earlier. The reconciliation framework gave the Congregation the right to request access to any investigation site that bore on the Calibration's documented harms. The coordinate was the most directly relevant site to the Calibration's documentation. Corin had approved the request and coordinated the access with the eastern communities' councils.

The session took place on a dry morning in early winter, six weeks before Lian's estimate of formation completion.

The Congregation arrived in two vehicles from the eastern settlement network. Vessa was there, in her formal representative capacity. Joren came from the Sanctuary — Corin had not seen Joren in eight months, and the eight months had been visible on him, not as decline but as the kind of change that comes from sustained difficult thinking. He had written two papers in that time, Vessa had mentioned in her relay. He had stopped writing them in a register that required the reader to already agree with his conclusions. Ash came from the Teller territory. Gull came alone, having traveled three days to reach the eastern communities.

It was Joren's first time in the eastern communities' territory. Corin had noted this in the field planning log when the attendance list was confirmed. The Congregation sessions at the Citadel — the formal proceedings, the evidence presentations, the reconciliation framework meetings — were where Joren had been present for the investigation's work over the preceding two years. He had read the investigation reports. He had asked questions during the formal sessions, technical questions, the kind that required precise answers. He had not been to the territory itself.

He was quiet when the vehicles arrived at the coordinate. He looked at the terrain — the flat ground, the tree line, the settlement at the horizon — for a long time before he approached the instrument. Corin watched him make the adjustment. The territory was different from reading about it. The coordinate stake in the flat ground was different from the investigation report's description of the coordinate stake in the flat ground. Joren stood at the perimeter and looked at the bore head and the instrument's transport case and then at the ground itself. He said nothing about the adjustment. He asked Lian, when he was ready, about the bore depth and the cable specification, and Lian answered him with the same precision she brought to any technical inquiry.

Bern was at the coordinate with three others from the eastern communities: Nela, who was one of the 216; Davan, who was one of the settlement council members; and a young man named Tav who was Orri's great-grandson and who had come without announcement, having heard from Bern that the session was happening.

The two groups met at the coordinate stake on a morning that was cold and still, the light flat and even across the terrain. Lian's team had set up the instrument for the observation phase — not for contact, Sable's signal was still in the minimum pattern — but for the formation-completion monitoring. The bore was open. The cable was at depth. The instrument's display showed the 65-meter signal in the stable minimum pattern it had held for five months.

The oversight committee observer was Petra, who had been designated as the committee's field liaison for the coordinate observation phase. She stood at the instrument's perimeter and did not participate in the Congregation session, per the mandate's separation of oversight and field investigation roles.

Corin stood between the two groups and explained what was below their feet. He had given this explanation to many audiences over the months — the oversight committee, the Congregation in formal session, the eastern communities' councils in the territory relay meetings. He had refined it. He knew which parts required more time and which parts registered quickly.

The formation at depth. Its function — the entity's perceptual architecture, the direct perceptual link between the entity's consciousness and the territory above. The damage. The repair. The 89% completion. The substrate normalization that would follow completion.

He explained the 216's relationship to the formation: the most-damaged formation, below the territory where the extraction array had operated, where the people who had permanently lost their Resonance architecture were living. The entity unable to perceive them directly for thirty-one years. Working toward them since the deployment.

Joren listened with his hands in his coat pockets. He asked three questions, technical questions about the formation's measurement methodology, and Lian answered them. Ash did not ask questions. She was looking at the ground.

Gull did not ask questions either. He stood at the coordinate stake for a long time after Corin's explanation was finished. Then he said: "I'm not resolved. But I'm present." He looked at Corin. "That is what I have."

Vessa said: "The Congregation accepts that."

Corin did not say anything to Gull. There was nothing to say that would help and he knew this from experience — the months of briefings had taught him the difference between situations that required a response and situations that required him to stay quiet and let the words stand on their own.

Gull looked at the ground. He was a large man, and he stood with the stillness of someone who had been standing in difficult places long enough to know how to hold the ground. He had come three days to stand here. He had not asked for anything from the standing. He was not waiting for the ground to give him something. He was present the way the investigation log required the presence of a witness — documenting the fact of being there, without claiming the fact resolved anything.

Corin wrote this into the session log that evening, as accurately as he could. He did not interpret what Gull's presence meant in terms of the Congregation's resolution process. That was for Vessa's record, not his. He wrote: *Gull present at the coordinate. Statement: not resolved, but present. No questions asked during the formal session.* He paused, then added: *traveled three days to attend.* That was a fact, not an interpretation, and it belonged in the record.

---

Bern stood at the coordinate stake after the Congregation's session ended and the visitors had moved toward the vehicles. He stayed. Davan and Nela stayed with him. Tav — Orri's great-grandson, who was seventeen and had said nothing during the full session — stayed also.

Corin waited.

Bern said: "The entity couldn't see us for thirty-one years."

"Not directly," Corin said. "Not through this formation."

Bern looked at the ground. He had been looking at the ground since the session began — not at the coordinate stake, at the ground itself, the soil and the dry winter vegetation and the flat terrain extending toward the settlement. He said: "She could feel something was missing."

Corin did not know if Bern meant Orri or the entity. He had thought about this possibility in advance — he had known that Bern's grandmother's death four months ago, and Bern's continued work in the eastern communities despite the grief, and Bern's presence at every investigation site session that the mandate allowed him to attend, meant that when Bern stood at the coordinate he was standing in the convergence of all of it. The grandmother who had died without seeing the investigation reach the Layer level. The ground that had felt absent for thirty-one years. The entity working below them toward something that might not arrive in time to mean anything to Orri but might mean something to Tav.

Bern had used the pronoun *she* for both. For Orri, always. But for the entity, he had stopped using a neutral term months ago — in the eastern community relay meetings, in his testimony updates, in his direct relays to the investigation team. He had started saying *she* for the entity and had not explained the choice and no one had asked him to explain it. The investigation record noted the entity without a pronoun. The investigation record was not Bern's.

"She could feel something was missing," Corin said. He accepted both readings. The sentence held both.

Bern did not respond to this. He stood for another minute at the stake. Then he said: "When the formation completes. What will we see? At the surface."

"We don't know exactly what substrate normalization looks like from the surface," Corin said. "The pre-Reckoner documentation describes the normalized substrate as a condition, not a visible event. The monitoring instruments will detect it. What people standing on the surface will observe or feel — that's not documented." He paused. "We'll be here when it happens. We'll find out."

Bern nodded. He turned and walked toward the vehicles.

Tav had not moved. He was still standing at the coordinate stake. He was looking at the ground the same way Bern had been looking at it.

Corin did not speak to him. He had noticed that Tav had come to listen, not to ask, and that the listening was the thing Tav had to contribute to the day. He let him stand.

---

Six weeks after the Congregation session, Lian came to Corin's office at 0700 with the monitoring data on her portable and said: "The formation is at 94%. The rate has accelerated again. I've recalculated completion." She looked at the portable's display. "Six weeks from today."

The basis for the estimate was visible in the monitoring data she had pulled up on her portable. The repair rate at 89% completion had been 16.2% per eight-week monitoring period. At 94% completion, the rate was 19.7% per eight-week period — still accelerating. The final integration of the peripheral fragments was operating faster than the outer-ring restoration had, which Lian's hypothesis ascribed to the formation's increasing structural coherence providing a stronger framework for the remaining integration. The intact center and the rebuilt inner rings were contributing to the repair of the final outer fragments in a way that had not been possible earlier in the process.

She had been measuring the rate at two-week intervals for the past two months. Each measurement required a bore deployment to the coordinate — a one-day operation, bore at dawn, cable at depth, mapping read at 65 meters, backfill by afternoon. She had made four such deployments in the past two months without a full team. Her and Wyne, and the bore team's minimal crew. The data was consistent across all four.

"What do you observe at the periphery that tells you the rate is increasing?" Corin asked.

"The signal density of the final peripheral fragments. In the outer zones of the formation — the last 3 meters of the 22-meter radius — the harmonic structure of the individual fragments has been increasing in coherence between measurements. The fragments are not just being integrated into the formation's structure by the entity's repair work at the periphery. They are exhibiting their own structural consolidation, as if the formation's field at that radius is doing some of the work. The formation is pulling itself together at the edges while the entity works from the inside out." She paused. "I didn't predict this. It is in the data. The mechanism is speculative, but the rate data supports the six-week estimate."

Corin pulled up the investigation calendar. Six weeks was a tight window for coordinating the observation team — Bern had been notified, the eastern communities' contacts had been notified in advance, but the oversight committee required thirty-day notice for field deployments and six weeks was thirty business days, barely within the notification window.

"I'll send the oversight notification today," he said.

"I'll prepare the instrument." She looked at the display. "I want to be at the coordinate when it happens."

"Yes," Corin said. "So do I."
