He sent the oversight notification. He sent the relay to Bern: *Six weeks estimated. Coordinate your travel with the field team's deployment schedule — I'll send the exact dates when the bore team is confirmed.* He sent the relay to Vessa and to the eastern communities' council.

Then he sent a relay he had been drafting for three days. To the oversight committee, directly to Elara Venn. The relay described the four-fragment transmission sequence, Sable's capacity decline, the current minimum state, and Lian's assessment that the fourth transmission was likely the last. The relay included the request: *The investigation recommends that the oversight committee formally acknowledge Sable's transmissions in the investigation record — not as evidence, for which the transmission content has already been entered and verified, but as a contribution to the investigation by a witness whose contribution had a cost. The oversight committee's acknowledgment is not required for the investigation to proceed. It is requested because the record should note that the transmissions were not provided without expense.*

He sent the relay. He did not know whether Venn would act on it. He had written it because it was true and because the record should contain it.

He returned to the formation monitoring data. 94% completion. Six weeks. The formation's outer edge integrating the last of its peripheral fragments. The entity's repair work, fourteen months running since the deployment, approaching the boundary where the formation would be whole.

Below the ground where Bern had stood. Below where Tav had stood. Below where Orri had walked for thirty-one years on ground that felt absent, and had died without seeing the absence addressed.

Six weeks.

chapters.title: Vol-016, Chapter 6: What the Entity Does
chapters.text:
The bore was complete by 0930. The cable was at depth by 1115. The instrument's display showed the formation at 98% completion on the first read at depth — the final peripheral fragments integrating at the outer edge of the 22-meter radius, the entity's repair work in its terminal phase.

The team at the coordinate: Lian, Corin, Iris, Fay. Two of Lian's monitoring team — Wyne, who had been on every eastern field deployment since the earliest fixed-array work, and Ren, who had joined the monitoring team after the mandate expansion and had not been to the coordinate before. Bern was there, with Nela and Tav. The oversight committee observer was Petra, standing at the instrument perimeter.

It was a still day. The winter light was flat and cold, the sky overcast but not threatening. The ground was dry. The coordinate stake was where it had always been. The instrument's surface unit was on its folding table. The generator hummed at the same frequency it had hummed at on every previous deployment.

Lian had told the team at the morning briefing: "I don't know what the display will show when the formation completes. The pre-Reckoner documentation describes the event in Layer-register terms, but I have not seen a formation complete in the primary register before. I will describe what the display shows when it shows it. I will not interpret in advance." She had paused. "I will also say: we don't know whether the formation completion will produce an observable surface event. The substrate normalization described in Sable's fourth fragment is a Layer-level process that propagates upward through the substrate. Whether the propagation produces anything detectable at the surface without instruments — that is not documented. It may not be visible or felt from the surface. The monitoring instruments will tell us what is happening."

Corin had briefed Bern separately, the same content. Bern had said: "I understand." He had brought Nela because Nela was one of the 216 and had asked to come. He had brought Tav because Tav had asked after the Congregation session and Bern had judged it right to bring him.

They all stood at the instrument perimeter at 1400, two and a half hours after the cable reached depth, watching the display.

The formation read at 98%. Lian logged it. At 1430: 99%. At 1447: the display shifted.

The formation monitoring display — the panel that showed the formation's structural integrity, translated from the depth-profile reads into a percentage and a structural diagram — reorganized. The percentage held for a moment at 99. The structural diagram, which had shown the formation's current extent in translucent blue against the formation's original extent in white, with the outer edge still showing the fragmentation that indicated ongoing repair — the structural diagram closed. The translucent blue filled the white.

The percentage shifted: 100.

Lian said: "It's complete."

The team was quiet.

The 65-meter signal on the reception display — the entity's direct signal in the Layer's primary register, the white and translucent blue frequencies that the instrument had been reading since the first deployment — reorganized into the pattern that the pre-Reckoner mapping had documented for intact formations. Not the repair pattern, not the working-toward pattern that the instrument had been reading for fourteen months. The formation's own native pattern, the pattern it had when it was intact and undisturbed and the entity was maintaining it in the ordinary way of eight centuries.

The team waited.

Wyne was watching the surface signal display — the shallow monitoring panel, which read the substrate at 20 meters. The substrate signal at 20 meters was at its normal baseline: amber, the familiar sinusoidal pattern at the same character it had been since the first deployment. Nothing changed in the substrate signal at 20 meters. Nothing changed in the 10-meter read. Nothing changed at the surface monitoring arrays' depth read.

For six hours: nothing.

Corin walked to the perimeter of the instrument setup at hour two and stood with Bern. Bern was watching the instrument. He had his hands in his coat pockets. He was very still.

"Nothing visible," Corin said.

"No," Bern said.

"The monitoring may show something that isn't visible. That may be what there is."

"I know," Bern said. He looked at the instrument display from where he stood, at a distance where the numbers weren't readable but the screen's illumination was visible. "I'm not expecting a visible event. I'm present." He paused. "It's enough to be present."

Nela was standing ten meters from the coordinate stake, looking at the ground. She had been standing in that position since the morning. She had not moved much. She was fifty-one. She had been twenty when the calibration operated. Corin did not know what she was thinking. He did not ask.

Tav was sitting on the ground near the vehicle bay, looking at the sky. He was the only one sitting. Everyone else was standing. Corin noticed this and thought about what it meant and decided it probably meant nothing specific except that Tav was seventeen and the day was long and sitting was a reasonable choice.

---

At hour seven, at 2114, Lian said: "The substrate signal has changed."

Corin was back at the instrument. He looked at the display.

The substrate signal at 20 meters was showing a new element. Not a replacement of the amber baseline — the baseline was still there, the familiar sinusoidal pattern unchanged. Below it: a sub-frequency. A new oscillation at a frequency lower than the substrate's baseline, stable and consistent and unlike anything in the monitoring archive's 23-year dataset.

Lian looked at the sub-frequency for a long moment. She logged it. She photographed the display. She activated the component isolation function and ran it at 20 meters — the same function she had used at depth to separate overlapping signals. The isolation separated the baseline substrate signal from the sub-frequency. The sub-frequency in isolation: a structured oscillation with a harmonic envelope that she had seen before.

"It has the entity's harmonic structure," she said. "Not the entity's substrate signal — the substrate signal is the mediated version, what the entity transmits through the ground. This is different. This is lower in the frequency range. The harmonic structure matches the entity's direct signal as I've read it in the Layer's primary register, but shifted into a substrate-compatible frequency range." She looked at the display for another moment. "The entity's direct signal, propagating through the substrate." She paused. "Substrate normalization."

She said it the way she said everything technical — as a description, not a declaration. But she said it twice. The first time to characterize the data. The second time, quietly, as if she was locating herself in relation to what she was seeing.

Bern had moved to the instrument display. Corin looked at him. Bern was looking at the sub-frequency reading on the substrate signal display — the amber baseline with the new element below it, the entity's direct signal in the substrate-compatible register, steady and consistent above the formation that had just completed its restoration.

The sub-frequency was in the territory above the formation. The eastern communities' territory. The place where the 216 lived. The place Orri had lived for thirty-one years and died four months ago without this.

Bern looked at the display for a long time.

He said: "She would have wanted to see this."

Corin said nothing. He looked at the display. He looked at Bern.

Bern said: "She knew it was coming. I think she knew it was coming." He looked away from the display. He looked at the coordinate stake. "She kept saying the ground would be different again someday. She said it for thirty years. People stopped asking her what she meant." He paused. "I never stopped asking. She said: it will be different when it's whole again. She didn't know the mechanism. She knew the direction." He was quiet for a moment. "I told her about the investigation. About the instrument. About what you found at depth. I told her as much as I could in the last months." He paused again. "She was glad. She said she was glad."

Corin had not known Orri had been told. Bern had not said this in any of the relay communications. He had kept it separate from the investigation record, which was appropriate — Orri's personal response to the investigation's findings was not investigation data. It was hers.

"I'm glad you told her," Corin said.

Bern nodded.

---

Iris was relaying. She had been relaying since Lian said *substrate normalization* — sending the update to Maret, to Bern's contact list at the eastern communities' councils, to Vessa Korr, to Joren, to Cross, to Elara Venn's office. The relay protocol she had established for the formation-completion event: confirmation of completion, then the substrate signal change, then the normalization characterization, then the investigation's current understanding of what normalization meant.

The relay she sent was the same to all recipients: *The formation completed at 1447. The substrate normalization sub-frequency appeared at 2114, seven hours after completion. The sub-frequency is stable and consistent, reading in the eastern communities' territory above the formation. We do not know what it means for the territory or the people in it. We are monitoring. We will document what we find.*

Fay was standing at the instrument perimeter when Iris sent the relay. She had been quiet for most of the day — she had been quiet for most of the month, in the specific contained way that she had been working since Orri's death, the way of someone who had grieved and returned to the work because the work was where she could do something with the grief. She looked at the instrument display and then she looked at the territory to the east — the direction of the settlement, the direction of the eastern communities, the direction of the ground above the formation.

She said: "What will the 216 feel?"

Lian said: "We don't know. The documentation describes the normalized substrate as the condition present before the harm. Whether that's detectable from the surface in a way a person could describe, we don't have a record of. The pre-harm period wasn't documented from the perspective of what it felt like to live in it." She paused. "We'll ask. We'll document what they say."

"Yes," Fay said.

She looked east for another moment and then she turned back to the instrument.

---

At midnight, Corin stood at the coordinate for the last time before the team broke down the equipment for the return. The sub-frequency in the substrate signal had been running for three hours, stable, unchanged. The formation's primary register reading was in the intact pattern, stable. Sable's signal at 65 meters was in the minimum pattern — still there, still the structured oscillation of a consciousness present in the Layer, but below the transmission threshold, unchanged from where it had been for five months.

He transmitted the recognition frequency at 65 meters.

It was reflex, not decision. He had done it at the first contact sequence, at the second, at the third. Each time the team was at depth, the practice had become a part of the coordinate's protocol. He transmitted the recognition frequency.

No response. The 65-meter signal was stable and unchanged. She could not respond. She could not even orient toward the transmission now — the capacity for that reorientation was spent. The signal at 65 meters was what remained after the transmissions: a minimal consciousness in the Layer, below the threshold of directed communication, present but unable to speak.

He held the transmission control for a moment after transmitting.

She was there. The signal was there, cycling in the white and translucent blue register of the Layer's primary frequency, the harmonic envelope that matched the Reckoner deprecated-minimal pattern, the structure of a consciousness that had been in the Layer for thirty-six months now, watching the entity's repair process from inside the medium the entity worked in. She had watched the formation complete. She had watched the sub-frequency begin.

He did not know what she experienced. He did not know if "experienced" was the right verb for whatever a Reckoner consciousness at minimum capacity, stabilized in the Layer's primary register, did with the signals it received. He had Lian's data and Sable's four fragments and his own institutional knowledge of the Reckoner network's architecture, and none of it answered the question of what it was like to be Sable at 65 meters on the night the formation completed.

He set the transmission control on the instrument's surface unit.

"She can't respond," Lian said from behind him. She had been watching.

"I know," he said.

"But you wanted to tell her," Lian said.

He looked at the display. The 65-meter signal, stable and present. "Yes," he said.

Lian said nothing more. She went back to the instrument's shutdown sequence.

---

He opened the investigation log on his portable. He had been writing the formation-completion entry in sections throughout the day, adding each piece of data as it arrived. Now he wrote the final section.

*Formation complete at 1447. Substrate normalization sub-frequency detected at 2114 — seven hours post-completion. Sub-frequency character consistent with entity's direct signal in substrate-compatible frequency range. Sub-frequency is stable and reading in the eastern communities' territory above the formation.*

*Sable's signal stable at 65 meters, minimum threshold. Below transmission capacity. Recognition frequency transmitted at coordinate. No response.*

He paused. He thought about what to add.

*The Layer formation at the Eastern Field Calibration coordinate is intact for the first time since the calibration event 31 years ago. The entity's perceptual link to the eastern communities' territory above is restored. The substrate normalization has begun. What it means for the 216 and for the territory is the primary open question of the next investigation phase.*

*The 42 formations across the region — 11 damaged, 31 intact. The 11 damaged formations represent 11 territories where the entity's perceptual link is severed and substrate normalization is absent. The coordinate formation's completion demonstrates that the repair process can succeed. The remaining 10 damaged formations represent the investigation's next scope.*

*Sable's four transmissions are the record's foundation for the Layer-level investigation. They were given at measurable cost. The record notes the cost.*

He read the entry back to himself. He made one addition, at the end:

*The investigation continues.*

He closed the log. He stood at the coordinate one more time. The instrument was in its shutdown sequence. The sub-frequency in the substrate was running below his feet, in the ground above the formation, in the territory the entity could now perceive. Lian was at the instrument. Iris was in the field shelter packing documentation. Bern and Nela and Tav were at the vehicles, in the dark, waiting for the team to finish.

He looked at the monitoring display — the formation at 100%, the normalization sub-frequency at the surface, the Sable signal at 65 meters, the restoration rate at 16.2% and rising.

The work was not finished. The ten remaining damaged formations required investigation, required field expeditions, required the third-generation instrument Lian was designing — the one that could reach 200 meters, that could read the Layer at depths the first-generation instrument couldn't touch. The forty-one intact formations required community testimony, required documentation of what the normalized substrate meant for the people who had been living in it without knowing what it was. The question of what substrate normalization meant for the 216 — the people above this specific formation, the people whose Resonance architecture was gone and could not be restored but who were now living above an intact formation in a normalized substrate for the first time in thirty-one years — required months of observation, required the investigation to stay at the coordinate and document what people said and felt and described.

The entity was doing something. The record would include it.

He walked to the vehicle. He got in. The team was there — Lian, Iris, Fay, Wyne, Ren, Petra. Bern's vehicle was ahead of them in the staging area, Bern and Nela and Tav already inside, waiting to convoy back to the eastern settlement for the night.

The coordinate was behind him. Below the ground: the formation at 100%, the entity's perceptual architecture intact for the first time in a generation, the sub-frequency beginning its propagation through the substrate above it. And at 65 meters, stable and present, the signal of a Reckoner consciousness that had chosen what to spend and had spent it and was still there, in the Layer, in the medium she had been built to monitor, in the environment that had sustained her when everything else had stopped.

The vehicle moved. The coordinate stake receded in the rearview mirror and then was gone, lost in the flat dark terrain of the eastern communities' territory.

The investigation continued.
